stem: The equation \( (2xy + [[a]])\,dx + (x^{2} + [[b]]y)\,dy = 0 \) is exact, so its solutions satisfy \( F(x,y) = C \) for a potential function \( F \). Find the constant \( C \) for the solution passing through \( (x,y) = ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \).
solution: Since \( M_y = 2x = N_x \), the equation is exact. Integrating \( M = 2xy + [[a]] \) in \( x \) gives \( F = x^{2}y + [[a]]x + h(y) \); matching \( F_y = x^{2} + [[b]]y \) forces \( h(y) = \frac{[[b]]}{2}y^{2} \). Thus \( F(x,y) = x^{2}y + [[a]]x + \frac{[[b]]}{2}y^{2} \). At \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), \( C = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor \( \tfrac12 \) when integrating \( [[b]]y \).
This omits the \( [[a]]x \) term of the potential.
This uses the wrong sign on the \( [[a]]x \) term.
This swaps the powers, using \( xy^{2} \) instead of \( x^{2}y \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: For what value of \( k \) does the equation \( \left(3x^{2}y + k y^{3} + ([[d]])x\right)\,dx + \left(x^{3} + [[m]]xy^{2} + ([[c]])\right)\,dy = 0 \) become exact?
solution: Matching \( M_y = 3x^{2}+3k y^{2} \) with \( N_x = 3x^{2}+[[m]]y^{2} \) forces \( 3k=[[m]] \), so \( k=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the coefficient \( [[m]] \) directly, forgetting to divide by 3.
This divides by 2 instead of 3.
This doubles the correct ratio.
This subtracts 3 from \( [[m]] \) instead of dividing by 3.
This divides by 6, double the correct divisor.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The equation \( \left(2xy + ([[a]])\right)\,dx + x^{2}\,dy = 0 \) is exact, with solution curves \( x^{2}y + ([[a]])x = C \). The curve through \( (x,y)=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) is followed to \( x=[[x1]] \). What is \( y \) there?
solution: The equation is exact (\( M_y=2x=N_x \)) with potential \( F=x^{2}y+[[a]]x \). Through \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), \( C=[[C]] \). At \( x=[[x1]] \): \( [[x1]]^{2}y+[[a]]\cdot[[x1]]=[[C]] \Rightarrow y=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square \( x_1 \) in the denominator.
This uses the wrong sign on the \( [[a]]x_1 \) term.
This drops the \( [[a]]x_1 \) term entirely.
This uses the original point's \( x_0 \) instead of \( x_1 \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The exact equation \( \left(2xy+([[a]])\right)dx + \left(x^{2}+[[b]]y\right)dy=0 \) has solutions satisfying \( x^{2}y+([[a]])x+\dfrac{[[b]]}{2}y^{2}=C \). For the member of this family with \( C=[[C]] \), which of the following is a value of \( y \) when \( x=[[x1]] \)?
solution: The equation is exact with potential \( F=x^{2}y+[[a]]x+\tfrac{[[b]]}{2}y^{2} \). Setting \( F([[x1]],y)=[[C]] \) gives \( \tfrac{[[b]]}{2}y^{2}+[[x1]]^{2}y+([[a]]\cdot[[x1]]-[[C]])=0 \); one root is \( y=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the quadratic \( y^2 \) term, treating the relation as linear in \( y \).
This has the sign flipped from the linear-only guess.
This drops the \( [[a]]x_1 \) term.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The equation \( [[cp]]y\,dx + ([[c]])x\,dy = 0 \) is not exact. Multiplying by an integrating factor \( \mu(x)=x^{n} \) makes the resulting equation exact. What is the value of \( n \)?
solution: Here \( M_y=[[cp]] \), \( N_x=[[c]] \), so \( \frac{M_y-N_x}{N}=\frac{[[c]]([[p]]-1)}{[[c]]x}=\frac{[[p]]-1}{x} \), a function of \( x \) alone. Thus \( \mu(x)=x^{[[p]]-1} \), i.e. \( n=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( p \) itself instead of \( p-1 \).
This adds 1 instead of subtracting.
This has the wrong sign.
This drops the constant term entirely.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The equation \( ([[c]])y\,dx + [[cq]]x\,dy = 0 \) is not exact. Multiplying by an integrating factor \( \mu(y)=y^{m} \) makes the resulting equation exact. What is the value of \( m \)?
solution: Here \( M_y=[[c]] \), \( N_x=[[cq]] \), so \( \frac{N_x-M_y}{M}=\frac{[[c]]([[q]]-1)}{[[c]]y}=\frac{[[q]]-1}{y} \), a function of \( y \) alone. Thus \( \mu(y)=y^{[[q]]-1} \), i.e. \( m=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( q \) itself instead of \( q-1 \).
This adds 1 instead of subtracting.
This has the wrong sign.
This drops the constant term entirely.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The equation \( \left([[k]]y\,e^{[[k]]xy} + [[a]]x\right)dx + \left([[k]]x\,e^{[[k]]xy} - [[a]]y\right)dy = 0 \) is exact. Find the constant \( C \) in \( F(x,y)=C \) for the solution through \( (x,y)=([[x0]],[[y0]]) \).
solution: Integrating \( M=[[k]]y e^{[[k]]xy}+[[a]]x \) in \( x \) gives \( F=e^{[[k]]xy}+\tfrac{[[a]]}{2}x^{2}+h(y) \); matching \( F_y=[[k]]x e^{[[k]]xy}+h'(y) \) with \( N \) forces \( h(y)=-\tfrac{[[a]]}{2}y^{2} \). At \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), \( C=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign on the \( y^{2} \) term.
This uses the wrong sign on the \( x^{2} \) term.
This flips the sign of both quadratic terms.
This omits the exponential term entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
